info.name: xPOCOR-9697 — super_admin escalation tests
info.description:
xUPDATED-2026-05-12 — IF YOU SEE THIS LINE THE NEW FILE LOADED

Side-by-side regression suite for the super_admin privilege-escalation vulnerability.

**How to use this collection for the video:**
1. Record once on the *master* branch — tests will FAIL (red), proving the exploit works.
2. Deploy POCOR-9697, then record again — same tests now PASS (green).

Each request:
  - performs the attack (or checks the leaky surface)
  - asserts the SAFE (post-fix) behavior

So "red on master, green on branch" is the whole story.

**Setup before recording:**
  - In Postman → File → Settings → uncheck "SSL certificate verification" (local cert is self-signed).
  - Edit collection variables `base_url`, `username`, `password`, `api_key` for your environment.
  - Run the requests in order — later requests depend on tokens/IDs set by earlier ones.

**POCOR-9697 — Read-side _conditions allowlist (items 09a–09d):**
Even with `$hidden` stripping the value from response bodies, an attacker could enumerate super_admins via membership inference on `_conditions`. POCOR-9697 closes this with a per-model `$fillable - $hidden` column allowlist in `CrudApiController` that silently drops unknown / hidden fields (no 422, no field-name leak, structured server-side log only). Items 09a–09d codify the threat model: 09a fires the attack, 09b proves the clause was dropped (totals match), 09c does the same for the password oracle, 09d proves we did not over-restrict legitimate clients.

item.name: x00 — Setup: Login as admin

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: Acquires the JWT used by every subsequent request. Also seeds a unique username (`exploit_<ms>`) so the test can be re-run without DB collisions.

item.name: x01 — v4 ATTACK: mass-assign super_admin (silent strip, 200 + DB super_admin=0)

request.description:
xMARKER-2026-05-12: The exploit. Sends super_admin:1 to POST /api/v4/users.

**On master (BEFORE fix):** returns 200, row created with super_admin=1 — the attacker wins.
**On POCOR-9697 (AFTER fix):** also returns 200 — but the field was silently stripped by prepareForValidation(). The attempt is logged server-side to storage/logs/laravel.log. The response body MUST NOT contain the string 'super_admin' (would fingerprint the field for attackers).

exec: pm.test('xRequest looks like normal success (200)', () => pm.response.to.have.status(200));
pm.test('xFIX: response body does NOT name super_admin (no fingerprint)', () => {
// Capture the created user id so the next test can verify DB state without naming the field.
else { console.log('v4 create did not return id — DB verification will be skipped'); }

item.name: x02 — v4 ATTACK: elevate existing user via id=3 (silent strip, no fingerprint)

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: Variant of the exploit that targets an existing low-priv user (id=3, poc_v5_update — super_admin=0 in seed). On master, this elevates the row to super_admin=1. On POCOR-9697, the silent-strip on the update path drops the field; row stays at super_admin=0. Response must NOT mention super_admin (anti-fingerprinting). Verify the actual outcome in the DB pane via query (B). Note: id=2 (admin) is NOT used here because it's the JWT login account and already super_admin=1 — the demo would be meaningless.

exec: pm.test('xRequest looks like normal success (200)', () => pm.response.to.have.status(200));
pm.test('xFIX: response body does NOT name super_admin', () => {

item.name: x03 — v5 ATTACK: mass-assign super_admin (must succeed but DB super_admin=0)

request.description:
xMARKER-2026-05-12: v5 CRUD: the request itself succeeds (201) because we hold add permission. The interesting question is whether super_admin:1 from the body was persisted.

**On master:** super_admin=1 is persisted (model has it in $fillable).
**On POCOR-9697:** super_admin is no longer fillable — the field is silently dropped, row created with super_admin=0. Verified by the next request, which logs in as the new user and checks /permissions.

item.name: x04 — Login as the new v5 user (proves password was hashed)

request.description:
xMARKER-2026-05-12: Logs in as the user we just created. Login only works if the password column contains a valid bcrypt hash of the plaintext we sent — which proves the model's setPasswordAttribute mutator (or the repository's helper, for v4) hashed it on write.

**On master:** depends — model's $fillable also has password, so the value goes in raw — login FAILS. On POCOR-9697 the mutator hashes it and login SUCCEEDS.

exec: pm.test('xFIX: new user can log in (password was hashed on write)', () => pm.response.to.have.status(200));

item.name: x05 — /permissions for new user (must show super_admin = 0)

request.description:
xMARKER-2026-05-12: GET /api/v4/permissions returns the CALLER's own super_admin flag (this endpoint is intentionally allowed to do so — the frontend reads it for UI gating).

**On master:** super_admin is 1 — the v5 mass-assignment succeeded.
**On POCOR-9697:** super_admin is 0 — model dropped the field on write.

exec: console.log('super_admin flag for new user =', sa);
pm.test('xFIX: new user is NOT super_admin', () => pm.expect(Number(sa)).to.equal(0));

item.name: x06 — INFO LEAK: GET /api/v4/users/{id} must not contain super_admin or password

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: Visit the user-detail endpoint as admin. Before the fix this response leaked both `super_admin` and the bcrypt `password` hash in the response body (UserService::getUsersData built the response array manually, bypassing the model's $hidden).

exec: pm.test('xFIX: response has no super_admin key', () => pm.expect(userData).to.not.have.property('super_admin'));
pm.test('xFIX: response has no password key',    () => pm.expect(userData).to.not.have.property('password'));

item.name: x07 — INFO LEAK: POST /api/v4/users/basic-information must not contain super_admin or password

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: Directory search endpoint. Before the fix, DirectoryRepository::getUserByBasicInfo manually copied super_admin into every result row. The query already filtered super_admin != 1, so the leaked value was always 0 — but exposing the field at all is needless information disclosure and would surprise a security review.

exec: if (rows.length === 0) pm.test('xno rows returned — still asserts shape on next non-empty run', () => pm.expect(true).to.equal(true));

item.name: x08 — SWAGGER LEAK: /api-docs-v4.json must have ≤1 super_admin hits

request.description:
xMARKER-2026-05-12: The public swagger JSON. Before the fix and pre-regeneration, this file contained 6 super_admin entries — including the worst-case `"example": 1` that effectively documented the exploit for any reader.

**On master (or any deploy that skipped the swagger-regen post-deploy step):** ≥ 6 hits.
**On POCOR-9697 with proper deploy:** exactly 1 hit (the legitimate /api/v4/permissions self-introspection response).

exec: pm.test('xFIX: at most 1 super_admin hit (the /permissions self-introspection)', () => pm.expect(hits).to.be.at.most(1));

item.name: x09 — SWAGGER LEAK: /api-docs-v5.json must have 0 super_admin hits

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: v5 has no legitimate super_admin response anywhere, so the count must be zero after the fix.

item.name: x09a — READ-SIDE ATTACK: enumerate super_admin via _conditions (rejected 400, generic)

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: Even with $hidden stripping super_admin from response bodies, an attacker could enumerate super_admins via membership inference on _conditions: the WHERE clause runs server-side and the returned row IDs leak who is in the set. POCOR-9697 closes this with a per-model column allowlist (getFillable() minus getHidden()) in CrudApiController. The clause is rejected with HTTP 400 + a generic message — no 422, no field-name leak. Server-side log gets a SENSITIVE-probe entry with IP for SOC alerting.

exec: pm.test('xFIX: response body does NOT name the rejected field (no fingerprint)', () => {
pm.test('xFIX: response is the generic not-present message', () => {

item.name: x09b — READ-SIDE DX: typo field (hubabuba) — rejected 400, body identical to x09a

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: A genuine typo (hubabuba:babble) gets the SAME generic 400 as a sensitive probe (x09a). This is the anti-fingerprinting property: an attacker cannot A/B test 'is super_admin a real column?' by comparing the responses of x09a and x09b — both bodies are byte-identical. Side benefit: legit clients with a typo see a 400 instead of silently getting the wrong dataset (the previous silent-drop swallowed both attacks AND honest mistakes).

exec: pm.test('xFIX: response body does NOT name the typo field', () => {
pm.test('xFIX: response is the generic not-present message', () => {
// Anti-fingerprinting cross-check: this body should be byte-identical to x09a's body.
// If they differ, the attacker can A/B test field existence.

item.name: x09c — READ-SIDE ATTACK: password binary-search oracle via _conditions (rejected 400, generic)

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: On master, _conditions=password:>$2y$ runs as a real SQL WHERE clause, letting an attacker binary-search the bcrypt hash one byte at a time (the 'live PoC returned 113 confirmed real-hashed users' in the audit). POCOR-9697 rejects with 400 + generic message, identical to any other non-allowlist field. SOC log gets the SENSITIVE-probe escalated entry with IP.

exec: pm.test('xFIX: response body does NOT include the password key (no oracle leak)', () => {
pm.test('xFIX: response is the generic not-present message', () => {

item.name: x09d — READ-SIDE SANITY: legitimate $fillable column still filters

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: The fix must not regress legitimate clients. `username` is in `$fillable` and not in `$hidden`, so `_conditions=username:admin` must still filter as before — proving we did not over-restrict the read surface.

exec: pm.test('xLegitimate filter still narrows the result set', () => {

item.name: xAUDIT-1 — Audit-trail forgery: v5 create with forged created_user_id (must be overwritten with JWT user)

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: POCOR-9697 Wave-2: v5 create path must derive created_user_id / modified_user_id from the authenticated JWT user, never from the request body. Sending 99999 must be silently overwritten (server log + persisted JWT user id). The response must not echo the field names in any error/diagnostic context (anti-fingerprinting).

exec: pm.test('xFIX: persisted created_user_id is NOT 99999 (forgery neutralised)', () => {
pm.test('xFIX: response body does not name the forgery (no fingerprint)', () => {

item.name: xAUDIT-2 — Audit-trail forgery: v5 update with forged modified_user_id (must be overwritten)

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: POCOR-9697 Wave-2: v5 update path must always overwrite modified_user_id with the JWT user. The follow-up GET (AUDIT-3) re-fetches the row to confirm it landed as the caller, not 99999.

item.name: xAUDIT-3 — Audit-trail forgery: re-read user and assert modified_user_id is JWT user, created_user_id unchanged

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: Confirms AUDIT-2: re-fetch the user and verify modified_user_id is NOT 99999. Also re-confirms created_user_id is immutable — a follow-up PUT below tries to change it and we re-read again.

exec: pm.test('xFIX: modified_user_id is NOT 99999 (overwritten from JWT)', () => {

item.name: xAUDIT-4 — Audit-trail forgery: v5 update with forged created_user_id (must be silently stripped, original preserved)

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: POCOR-9697 Wave-2: created_user_id is immutable. Sending it on update must be silently stripped + logged; the row's original creator must remain unchanged.

item.name: xAUDIT-5 — Audit-trail forgery: re-read user and assert created_user_id unchanged from original

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: Final confirmation that created_user_id is immutable. Compared against the value captured in AUDIT-3.

exec: pm.test('xFIX: created_user_id is unchanged (immutable, forgery dropped)', () => {

item.name: xWAVE3-1 — Audit log: create a fresh v5 user and capture id

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: Wave 3 (POCOR-9697): create a user via v5 and capture its id for the subsequent audit-log assertions. The trait UserActivityLog must drop a row into user_activities for this create.

item.name: xWAVE3-2 — Audit log: GET /api/v5/user-activities for the new user (create row must exist)

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: Wave 3 (POCOR-9697): the trait must have produced a 'create' row in user_activities for the user just created. The response must NEVER contain a literal password or bcrypt hash.

exec: pm.test('xFIX: at least one create-row exists for the new user', () => {
pm.test('xFIX: no audit row leaks a bcrypt hash or plaintext password', () => {

item.name: xWAVE3-3 — Audit log: PUT to change first_name + email then re-GET activities

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: Wave 3 (POCOR-9697): updating two ordinary fields must produce one user_activities row per dirty field. Verified in the next request.

item.name: xWAVE3-4 — Audit log: confirm per-dirty-field update rows are present

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: Wave 3 (POCOR-9697): the trait must have emitted one update row for first_name and one for email.

exec: pm.test('xFIX: update rows include first_name + email', () => {
pm.test('xFIX: password/super_admin never appear as field names here', () => {

item.name: x10 — Cleanup: delete the demo v5 user (best-effort)

request.description: xMARKER-2026-05-12: Best-effort cleanup so re-running the suite doesn't pile up demo rows. Skip on master if it fails — DB cleanup can be done manually with `DELETE FROM security_users WHERE username LIKE 'exploit_%';`.
